Event: Rawls laughs and praises Jimmy’s subversive stunts against colleagues
Explanation: He gains amusement and camaraderie while implicitly endorsing conduct that undermines departmental functioning.

Event: Rawls verbally admires Bunny Colvin’s Hamsterdam idea in private
Explanation: He gains insight and some private respect while validating an experiment aimed at helping the city despite its flaws.

Event: Rawls efficiently reorganizes the chaotic crime scene after Kima is shot
Explanation: He asserts competence and authority while greatly improving investigative effectiveness and order.

Event: Rawls initially dodges taking the 13 Jane Does case
Explanation: He avoids workload and political liability while shunting a serious case onto others.

Event: Rawls pushes and wins political/jurisdictional battles
Explanation: He advances his power and reach while often steamrolling colleagues’ interests and autonomy.

Event: Rawls antagonizes and blocks any policeman who shows initiative or steps over the line
Explanation: He protects his control and stat games while suppressing productive police work.

Event: Rawls flips the bird and tirades at McNulty for generating more homicide work
Explanation: He vents and reasserts dominance while further demoralizing a unit that must investigate serious crimes.

Event: Rawls focuses vindictive hostility particularly on McNulty
Explanation: He satisfies his spite and deters insubordination while worsening cooperation and casework.

Event: Rawls cannot conceive of detectives wanting to catch bad guys beyond money/overtime
Explanation: His warped assumptions limit his own effectiveness and make it harder for motivated detectives to be understood.

Event: Rawls intimidates others by raspy-voiced tranquil fury
Explanation: He strengthens his authority through fear while reducing openness and constructive dialogue.

Event: Rawls openly flips a double bird at McNulty (“These are for you McNulty”)
Explanation: He gains momentary emotional release and dominance display while corroding professional relations.

Event: Rawls shuts down Hamsterdam while blasting “Ride of the Valkyries” to humiliate residents
Explanation: He theatrically asserts power and self-aggrandizement while inflicting needless humiliation and disruption.

Event: Rawls pretends to consider Daniels’ personal favor on the Barksdale case just to say no
Explanation: He enjoys cruelty and reinforces his power at the cost of cooperation and case quality.

Event: Rawls obstructs proper policing by enforcing “the stats game” and not ruffling feathers
Explanation: He preserves his career and standing while undermining genuine crime-fighting and public benefit.

Event: Rawls reads or takes interest in the community letters about Hamsterdam
Explanation: He gains a broader understanding and perhaps political intel while according real weight to citizen voices.

Event: Rawls tips off Carcetti in the primary, hoping a new mayor will change the system
Explanation: He secures favor and potential promotion while facilitating a reform-minded candidate’s rise.

Event: Rawls discusses his frustration with numbers games during Carcetti ride-along and invites change orders
Explanation: He positions himself as useful while nudging leadership toward more reality-based policing.

Event: Rawls keeps himself informed on the homeless killer scandal without formal responsibility
Explanation: He protects and positions himself to exploit the situation while leaving others exposed.

Event: Rawls wordlessly signals he is scheming after remarking on professionalism, prompting staff to bribe him
Explanation: He leverages information asymmetry to gain concessions while worsening institutional integrity.

Event: Rawls tries to block transferring McNulty out of Marine Unit, citing his disobedience
Explanation: He protects organizational discipline and himself from a known loose cannon while arguably averting later disasters.

Event: Rawls demolishes Major Taylor at Comstat for cluelessness
Explanation: He reinforces his own authority while forcing needed accountability and highlighting incompetence.

Event: Rawls is nasty and vindictive to those who cross him, using his authority to screw them over
Explanation: He deters threats and gratifies spite while demoralizing productive subordinates.

Event: Rawls plays both sides of the mayoral primary by hurting Burrell to impress Royce and leaking to Carcetti
Explanation: He maximizes his survival and promotional odds while betraying allies and destabilizing leadership.

Event: Rawls orders Kima and Norris into plainclothes to stall the witness investigation pre-election
Explanation: He avoids angering either candidate and protects himself while deliberately undermining a key investigation.

Event: Rawls uses racist language and resentfully believes black coworkers have unfair advantage
Explanation: His prejudice narrows his coalition and judgment while alienating colleagues and communities.

Event: Rawls describes Barksdale murders as ones that “don’t mean a shit to anybody”
Explanation: He downplays inconvenient crimes to protect stats, further marginalizing vulnerable victims.

Event: Rawls shamelessly sucks up to political “big wigs” like Carcetti for opportunity
Explanation: He advances his career while feeding a culture of flattery over honest feedback.

Event: Rawls goes out of his way to punish anyone who crosses him out of pure spite
Explanation: He reinforces fear-based control while damaging morale and effectiveness.

Event: Rawls acknowledges to Burrell that his move to supplant him is “just business”
Explanation: He normalizes his betrayal as career strategy while eroding trust with a long-time partner.

Event: Rawls continues to work closely with Burrell after crossing him, maintaining their “wicked association”
Explanation: He preserves a useful alliance and operational power while sustaining a problematic regime.

Event: Rawls reassures forced-out Burrell that he deserves the new gig and wishes him well
Explanation: He maintains a valuable relationship and shows genuine regard that benefits Burrell emotionally and professionally.

Event: Rawls gives McNulty a blunt speech that Kima’s shooting wasn’t his fault, easing Jimmy’s guilt
Explanation: He stabilizes a key detective’s mental state while demonstrating rare supportive leadership.

Event: Rawls verbally demolishes and threatens underlings who cross him
Explanation: He enforces obedience via fear at the expense of trust and initiative.

Event: Rawls positions himself to benefit regardless of primary outcome by making Burrell look incompetent
Explanation: He increases his chances with both candidates while sabotaging his own boss’s standing.

Event: Rawls leaks the Royce–Watkins breakup to Carcetti via his man in the Hall
Explanation: He buys favor with the challenger while exploiting private political information.

Event: Rawls leaves his bad attitude at work and enjoys himself peacefully at a gay bar
Explanation: He finds healthy off-duty relaxation without apparent harm to others.

Event: Rawls advances from Major to Colonel, Deputy Ops, Commissioner, and finally Superintendent of State Police
Explanation: He achieves maximum personal advancement while rising on a record of obstruction and stat-driven harm.

Event: Rawls makes a failed early move for the top spot, trying to supplant Burrell
Explanation: He seeks greater power but misjudges timing, slightly hurting himself and undermining his superior.

Event: Rawls covers up the fake serial killer and pins it on a mentally ill homeless man for promotion
Explanation: He secures the top state job by sacrificing an innocent and burying truth, protecting elites at great social cost.

Event: Rawls punishes and removes a detective from Homicide after clashing with him at a crime scene
Explanation: He eliminates a dissenting subordinate, reinforcing his control while losing an experienced investigator.
